data.title: Astec Industries: Shares Still Offer More Upside Despite Revenue Falling
data.description:
Back in late April of this year, I decided to award Astec Industries (NASDAQ:ASTE) with a rare double upgrade, popping it from a "Sell" rating to a "Buy" rating. I can probably count on one hand the number of times I have done this in my career. In the past, I had been turned off by the company because of a combination of weakening financial performance and how the stock was valued. And during the time that I was bearish, the company did in fact underperform the S&P 500. Since upgrading it, however, we have seen some great results. While the market is up 21.6%, shares of this business, which produces, sells, and services equipment and components used in asphalt and concrete road-building and related construction activity, have shot up 34.3%.

After such a nice move higher over a relatively short period, you might think that I would argue for a rating downgrade. But honestly, even though we have seen some weakness in sales as of late, the overall picture for the company is getting better. And when you consider how cheap the firm is as of this writing, I think that maintaining a "Buy" rating is entirely appropriate.

A solid business at a good price

A lot is going on these days when it comes to Astec Industries. For starters, recent financial performance provided by management has been rather mixed. During the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, for instance, revenue came in at $330.3 million. That represents a decline of 4.4% compared to the $345.5 million the company reported the previous year. This decline in sales was attributable to lower volumes and changes in product mix that negatively affected it. It was only because of higher prices that revenue wasn't even lower on a year-over-year basis.

Crucially, international sales for the company fell the hardest, dropping 6.9% from $73.4 million to $68.3 million because of lower demand for its equipment and a reduction in parts and component sales of about $2 million. In the domestic market, things were only a bit better, with revenue falling 3.7% from $272.1 million to $262 million. As was the case in international markets, lower equipment sales negatively affected the business, as did a reduction in component sales, as well as service and equipment installation activities.

Even though revenue for the company declined, overall profitability for the business improved. It went from a net loss of $14 million in the second quarter of last year to a net gain of $16.7 million this year. To be clear, a large chunk of this stemmed from the $20.2 million impairment charge the company booked in the second quarter of last year that did not repeat this year. But even adjusted net profits improved, growing from $14 million to $20.3 million. Other profitability metrics improved without exception. Take operating cash flow as an example. Year over year, it increased from $10.9 million to $12.9 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $16.2 million to $30.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $27.6 million to $33.7 million.

For the first half of 2025 in its entirety, revenue for the company did actually inch up from $654.7 million to $659.7 million. And as that happened, profitability for the business improved markedly. This can be seen in the chart above, with net profits, adjusted net profits, and all cash flow metrics that I looked at for the company improving nicely on a year-over-year basis. This brings me to another thing that has been going on for quite some time now that seems to finally be bearing fruit.

Management has been working on a strategic transformation program aimed at implementing a standardized ERP system that should drastically improve its core financial systems that have up to this point been disparate. Even though this may not sound like much of an endeavor, it is typically massive in terms of the investment needed and the time required to complete. This is a multi-year initiative that management intends to conclude in 2028 or 2029 and that will cost between $180 million and $200 million. However, the company has already incurred $143 million of those implementation expenses. The firm has also been working to mitigate the impact of tariffs, including increasing prices on its customers, changing some of its sourcing from other countries, mostly from China to India, reshoring when and where possible, and making adjustments to its manufacturing footprint.

But the biggest change, at least in my opinion, is the company’s purchase of TerraSource Holdings that was completed on July 1 of this year at a price of $245 million. But after factoring in the net present value of anticipated tax benefits, this price drops to $230 million. This was a deal that I talked about in my previous article about the firm. In essence, TerraSource Holdings operates as a producer and distributor of equipment that is very similar to what Astec Industries produces and distributes and that caters to the materials processing equipment and aftermarket parts spaces. In addition to meaningfully growing operations, this purchase opens the door for cost savings. Total annualized EBITDA generated by TerraSource Holdings should be around $29 million. But when we factor in potential synergies of $10 million, we get a reading of $39 million. These figures are based off the $150 million in annual revenue that the enterprise brings in.

Thanks to this purchase, as well as stronger-than-expected financial results, the company has revised higher its expectations for the 2025 fiscal year. They previously expected EBITDA, for instance, to be $110 million at the low end and $125 million at the high end. Their previous low-end estimate was $105 million. But in addition to this, the company also anticipates EBITDA from TerraSource to be between $13 million and $17 million. This half-year of results should push EBITDA for Astec Industries to between $123 million and $142 million.

Of course, it's not good enough to evaluate the company using half a year of results for this latest purchase. So on a pro forma basis, I'm baking in the entire year, which should give us a midpoint EBITDA of around $147 million. That should translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $134.6 million. If we use these estimates, and we also factor in the same pro forma figures for 2024, we would be able to see in the chart above how shares of Astec Industries are valued on a pro forma basis. These multiples, specifically for the 2025 estimates, look very attractive to me. And relative to other similar firms, the picture isn't bad. In the table below, I compared Astec Industries to five other similar firms. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper. And on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, three of the five are cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Astec Industries 7.9 8.8
Douglas Dynamics (PLOW) 16.2 13.9
Mueller Industries (MLR) 9.6 6.8
Wabash National (WNC) 4.5 4.4
The Manitowoc Company (MTW) 25.4 7.6
Wabtec (WAB) 19.5 15.8

Even though I am taking a bullish stance on the firm, one risk that needs to be considered is declining backlog, which could lead to weaker revenue in the future. As you can see in the image below, a plunge in backlog associated with its infrastructure operations has pushed overall backlog down over time. This is something that needs to be monitored as time goes on.

Takeaway

Based on the entirety of the picture that we have right now, I would say that there is more upside potential that exists for shareholders of Astec Industries. This latest acquisition provides a nice surge in expected profitability. And even without factoring in the prospect of $10 million of annual synergies, which will take time to realize if they are realized at all, the company looks attractively priced on an absolute basis and looks decent relative to other similar firms. Given this combination of factors, I believe that maintaining a "Buy" rating is the right choice.

data.title: Intel's Nvidia Backing May Not Be Enough
data.description:
Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) is the sixth-largest semiconductor firm in the world. It used to manufacture PC microprocessors and has pushed deeper into data centers, AI, and chip manufacturing now. Recently, Intel has secured nearly $17 billion in backing, which includes $8.9 billion from the U.S government to support domestic chip production, $5 billion from Nvidia for their AI-focused collaboration, and $2 billion from Softbank.

Through the new collaboration with Nvidia, Intel will create special CPUs and all-in-one chips that incorporate Nvidia’s powerful graphics tech. It means that Intel’s chips will be able to handle both everyday computer tasks and advanced graphics or AI tasks in the same package. By combining the strength of Intel’s CPU and NVIDIA’s GPUs, the two companies can make efficient products for large data centers, businesses, and even personal computers.

While focusing on making the best use of the new investments, Intel is tightening its operations. The company has lowered its full-year expense target to $16.8 billion, spinning off its Altera programmable unit with Silver Lake, through a $200 million cost reduction deal. Although Intel recorded a $2.9 billion loss in the first half of 2025, it still managed to make $12.9 billion in revenue, exceeding analysts’ expectations. Under CEO Lip-Bu Tan, the company plans to double down on U.S. operations and scale back on some overseas projects, and it could be strategically important for the company.

Bearing in mind the recent news regarding INTC, we need to analyze which of these factors will have a long-term and continuous effect on the growth of the company and its share price. The recent funding from the government could mean expansion, but pulling back from overseas projects could backfire.

It is imperative to analyze the fundamentals of the company so that the current news doesn’t manage to entirely sway our opinion about investing in the company.

Snapshot of the Company

Intel has a company rating score of 43 out of 100. It appears to have below-average fundamentals, with several negative figures. It raises concerns about the overall financial health of the company.

Fundamentals

First, let’s look over the price per share history. In the chart below, we can see that the price per share has an inconsistent trend, with the price dropping drastically in 2023 and 2024, followed by a sudden surge in 2025. This shows that the company has volatile cycles, even though the price was consistently increasing until 2022. This might have been due to loss of market share and weak revenue growth, especially in those two years. Possibly because rivals like AMD, Nvidia, and ARM-based players gained ground. Also, demand for PCs lowered as AI and accelerated computing became more central.

Overall, the share price average has grown by about 45.57% over the past 10 years, or a Compound Annual Growth Rate of 4.26%. This is a modest return, showing some growth but far below what I would consider as a high-growth tech stock.

Earnings

If we look closely at Intel’s earnings history, we see a very different story today compared to its earlier years of stability. From 2015 to 2021, the company’s diluted earnings per share showed a gradual overall growth, rising from $2.33 to $4.86. For most of this period, Intel dominated in sales of PC processors and efficiently met data center requirements and global demands for its semiconductor technology. Maintaining its EPS levels between 2017 and 2021 for four consecutive years is proof of Intel’s strength during that time.

However, from 2022 onwards, Intel’s earnings started to weaken significantly. EPS dropped from $1.94 to just $0.4 in two years, and ultimately fell to negative numbers, with a loss of -$4.38 per share in 2024. There were many factors behind this steep decline, including the manufacturing delays, restructuring charges, and heavy competition in AI, which the company was facing. Another possible reason was setbacks and rising costs in its global expansion, which led to financial strain.

Intel’s earnings today and its volatility over the years make it difficult for investors to predict the company’s future performance with confidence. Intense competition in this industry and risks associated with Intel’s current execution plans make the situation even more uncertain. While Intel has proven to deliver strong earnings during favorable cycles, the sharp downfall in 2022 shows that the company requires a cautious outlook.

Return on Equity

The return on equity for INTC has consistently decreased from 2020 to 2024, taking a drastic downfall from 26.36% to a negative territory at-18.31%. This highlights the financial strain caused by recent losses and competitive pressure.

For return on equity (ROE), I hope for a 5-year average of 16% or more. For Intel, it is 8.07%, which is well below the benchmark. This suggests that, on average, the company has struggled to generate strong returns on equity and finds it challenging to maintain earnings. So, INTC doesn’t meet my requirements.

Let's compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of semiconductor companies is 19.08%. INTC’s 5-year average of 8.06% is well below the industry average.

Return on Invested Capital

The return on invested capital has also been a consistent decliner from 2020 to 2024. It went from 18.93% to -12.73%, which is a very concerning trend. The 5-year average ROIC stands at 5.79%. For return on invested capital (ROIC), I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, INTC does not pass the test. The trend indicates that Intel has not been able to create value from its invested capital.

Gross Margin Percent

Intel’s gross margin percent (GMP) has steadily decreased over the last five years. This downward trend indicates mounting cost pressure and weaker pricing power. The 5-year average GMP of 45.35% is good, though. I typically look for companies with gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So, INTC has proven that it has the ability to reach high margins, but its declining trend is worrisome.

Financial Stability

When we take a look at other fundamentals relating to Intel’s balance sheet, we can see that the debt-to-equity ratio is less than 1. It is marked at 0.52, indicating that the company has nearly double the equity compared to its debt.

INTC’s current ratio of 1.24 is also satisfactory; it reflects that the company can use its current assets to pay its short-term liabilities. Any number over one is considered to be acceptable for current ratio.

To sum up the balance sheet, INTC has enough assets to pay for the debts, if it comes to that. And in the short term, it is generating enough cash from operations to fulfil day-to-day requirements.

Intel currently offers a dividend yield of just 0.61%, which is low. This indicates that investors shouldn’t expect much of an income from dividends, at the moment.

An analysis of the value of the company vs. the share price would help us gain more insight about where the business stands right now.

Value Vs. Price

Intel doesn’t have a reported Price Earnings (P/E) ratio because recent earnings are negative. It is difficult to assess whether INTC is overvalued or undervalued using the P/E ratio.

The estimated value of stock is $8.31, and the current stock price of INTC is $36.02. This indicates that this stock is currently selling way above the estimated value, and is therefore not worth buying at this time.

Keep in mind that Intel is a very difficult company to estimate stock value for at this time, because of the company’s volatility, negative earnings, and lack of P/E ratio.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

Based on the facts, Intel shows a mix of stability and challenges. The company can be considered financially secure if we consider the fact that it has sufficient equity relative to its debt and enough short-term assets to cover its day-to-day obligations. The gross margins are also a positive indicator and show that the company has achieved high margins over the past five years.

However, recent profitability trends show some serious issues. Return on equity and return on invested capital both have fallen sharply and are in negative territory at the moment. It suggests that Intel is struggling to generate a good and consistent value from its operations. Earnings have been volatile and are currently negative. It could be because of challenges from global expansions and setbacks related to those issues, and other factors, including high competition. It is difficult to predict Intel’s future performance with confidence.

We know that Intel has secured substantial investment recently and is planning to collaborate with Nvidia on AI-focused products. But the impact these initiatives will have on the long-term growth of the company remains uncertain. We can say that cutting costs and focusing on U.S. operations are strategic moves that can help stabilize performance, but there is a lot of competitive pressure and big risks associated with the execution of these plans.

Overall, the ups and downs of the company suggest that investors should approach cautiously. And with an absence of reported P/E ratio and negative earnings, most valuation methods can’t be relied upon. That being said, with the information that we do have, it seems that Intel is overpriced.

INTC Vs. The S&P 500

Now, let’s see how INTC compares to the U.S. stock market benchmark S&P 500 over the past 10 years.

From the chart below, we can see that INTC has significantly underperformed the general market. While the S&P 500 continued its steady growth, Intel’s stock began to slow down and decline around 2022. Even with recent investments and partnerships, the stock remains well below its historical peak and is way behind in the broader market’s performance.

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Over the next five years, the analysts who follow this company are expecting it to grow earnings at an average annual rate of 9.77%.

In addition, the average one-year price target for this stock is $24.87, which is about a 30.05% increase in a year.

Does INTC Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? NO (43)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? NO (4.26%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? NO
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? NO (8.07%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? NO (5.79%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? YES (45.35%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? YES
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? YES
  9. Outperformed the S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? NO
  10. Do I think this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? NO

INTC scored 3/10 or 30%. Therefore, investors need to be careful while considering INTC as a potential investment.

Is INTC currently selling at a bargain price?

  1. Price Earnings less than 16? Cannot Be Determined (because of negative earnings)
  2. Estimated Value greater than the Current Stock Price? NO (Value $8.31 < $29.34 Stock Price)

For me, the choice is clear. I will certainly not consider adding INTC to my watchlist of stocks at this time because it fails to meet several criteria on my checklist. While the company has strong backing now and has entered into important partnerships, its fundamentals, such as negative earnings, declining profitability, and underperformance in the market, suggest noteworthy risks.

The dividend yield isn’t promising either, so there is a very low chance of meaningful income from dividends on this stock. The stock also appears to be overvalued compared to its estimated value, so investing now would likely carry a high amount of risk for a relatively low return potential.

data.title: Crinetics: FDA Approval And Expansion Of Palsonify In Focus, Maintaining 'Buy' Rating
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:CRNX) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Crinetics: Earlier Data Release For CAH Treatment Bodes Well For Upcoming Conference Data". With respect to this article, I noted the company's development of its drug atumelnant for the treatment of patients with congenital adrenal hyperplasia [CAH]. Another item I noted was that the company was gearing up to submit its New Drug Application [NDA] of paltusotine for the treatment of patients with Acromegaly in the 2nd half of 2024. Since then, there have been major developments in terms of this program advancement. The first of which is that the FDA accepted the company's submission of its NDA for paltusotine for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly and set a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of September 25, 2025.

The second of which is that just the other day it was revealed that the FDA approved this drug for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. This drug is to be marketed under the name of PALSONIFY. This regulatory approval is highly important because it changes the scope of the treatment paradigm for these patients in two ways. One way is that currently available therapies are injectable, while its drug is given as a once-daily oral option. As a matter of fact, it is the first daily oral therapy option to treat these patients with Acromegaly. Secondly, patients receiving current treatment options had something known as symptom breakthroughs, and PALSONIFY covers this as well. If FDA approval was achieved, why do I believe it is still important to go over this biotech? That's because there are two other shareholder value generating events still in play.

The first of which is that the company had already submitted its Marketing Authorization Application [MAA] of paltusotine for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly to the European Union [EU]. A decision beforehand from the Committee for Medicinal Products and Human Use [CHMP] with respect to this MAA is expected in the 1st half of 2026. The second of which is an expansion opportunity to develop paltusotine for the treatment of another indication, which is patients with carcinoid syndrome. The use of this somatostatin receptor agonist targeting these patients is being explored in the phase 3 CAREFNDR study. All of this value doesn't even consider the phase 3 CAH treatment program of studies using atumelnant targeting patients with CAH. Two phase 3 studies for this program are expected to begin in the 2nd half of 2025.

FDA Approval Of PALSONIFY Bodes Well For Large Underserved Target Market

As I noted above, Crinetics Pharmaceuticals received FDA approval of its somatostatin receptor type 2 [SST2] nonpeptide agonist PALSONIFY for the treatment of patients with Acromegaly. This FDA approval occurred because of positive results from two phase 3 pivotal trials, which were PATHFNDR-1 and PATHFNDR-2. The thing is that the company saw excellent trial results from these two pivotal trials, which were biochemical and symptom controls of disease. For instance, in the phase 3 PATHFNDR-1 study, it was noted that the primary endpoint of proportion of patients who ended up achieving IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0 xULN was achieved in a statistically significant manner with a p-value of p <0.0001 [36-week period]. About 83% of patients who were given PALSONIFY achieved biochemical control of IGF-1 levels at the end of the study. On the flip side, only 4% of placebo patients were able to achieve this measure. In addition, it saw a rapid onset response of this biochemical IGF-1 level as early as 2-4 weeks and maintained it throughout the entire study. This handles what I noted about the biochemical control of this specific late-stage study.

It is also important to immediately point out that the PATHFNDR-1 study was made in which patients switched to PALSONIFY from the standard of care [SOC] treatments they were on, which is typically an injectable somatostatin receptor ligand [SRL] . What about symptom control? Well, in this regard, there was the deployment of the efficacy endpoint measure change from baseline of total Acromegaly Symptoms Diary [ASD] score. This particular score measures a patient's daily life in terms of symptoms that they experience with this disorder. This includes symptom scores for headaches, swelling, joint pain, and many others incorporated into this endpoint measure. The point is that the score ranges from "0" all the way to "70" overall. However, each of these items I just laid out are scored with "1" being best all the way to "10" as worst. Thus, the higher the score, the worse off a patient is in terms of symptom severity. When patients entered the study they had an ASD score ranging from 11 to 13, which indicates that symptoms were present. With that being said, 30 patients given once-daily oral paltusotine achieved a -0.6 point reduction in this ASD measure from baseline, while 28 patients who were given placebo achieved a +4.6 worsening [Crinetics Corporate Presentation August 2025, slide #11]. In terms of Breakthrough Symptom Score, a post-hoc analysis concluded that frequency of breakthrough symptoms was reduced dramatically over a 6 - 9 month treatment period.

As I stated in the beginning above, Crinetics pharmaceuticals' ability to receive FDA approval of PALSONIFY for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly is only the beginning. Why do I state that? That's because the company had already submitted another regulatory application of this drug to target these patients with the European Union [EU]. In essence, a Marketing Authorization Application [MAA] is already currently underway for review by this agency. Even better, this specific mode that was made, brings an important catalyst opportunity. It is expected that the Committee for Medicinal Products and Human Use [CHMP] is expected to give their opinion of this MAA for paltusotine in the the 1st half of 2026.

Acromegaly is characterized as the production of excessive growth hormone [GH] by a patient's pituitary gland. A benign pituitary tumor can be a cause to excrete excess GH. With this happening from the pituitary gland, it leads the liver to develop excess IGF-1 levels. Why are these excess secretions of hormone levels bad? That's because it can cause overgrowth of bones and tissues in the patient's body, which in turn leads to the several symptoms that they end up experiencing. Some symptoms that these patients can experience as a result of this disorder are as follows:

  • Enlarged heart
  • Large bones of several parts of the body
  • Decreased muscle strength
  • Fatigue
  • Blood Pressure increase
  • Thyroid enlargement
  • Many other burdensome symptoms

The global Acromegaly treatment market size is projected to reach $2.37 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity and it remains to be seen if it will ultimately be able to capture a huge chunk of it. I believe it can, and this is based on its mechanism of action [MOA], which allowed it to achieve both biochemical and symptom control in its clinical testing. Basically, PALSONIFY is a differentiated type of somatostatin analog in that it targets the SST2 receptor responsible for excessive secretion of GH. Why target this specific receptor? That's because it is believed to be the most potent target. The binding of the SST2 receptor is important to reduce IGF-1 levels, which is a hormone that promotes growth. Beyond the mode of action, the company was able to make a significant stride over other currently approved standard of care [SOC] drugs like somatostatin receptor ligands [SRLs] octreotide and lanreotide. These depot injections of peptide SRLs fail to control symptom burden. For instance, it is said that roughly 52% of patients say that their Acromegaly symptoms worsen at the end of a cycle of treatment. This, along with the site injection reaction burden, is problematic. An oral pill like PALSONIFY, a first of its kind for these patients, could achieve superior patient compliance for starters. In addition, to potentially performing in a superior fashion when it comes to breakthrough symptom burden they have to deal with.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Crinetics Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents and investment Securities of $1.2 billion as of June 30, 2025. The truth is that this is an ideal company to look into because it has plenty of cash on hand. This is not just based on an estimate I believe to be true, this is what the company notes in its second quarter 2025 financial results. It believes that with the cash on hand, it can fund its operations into 2029. With this projection, it has enough cash on hand to make it through many clinical milestones over the next several years. However, if the company does need to eventually raise cash to keep itself funded, it already has a financial tool in place at its disposal.

This would be with respect to a 2024 ATM Offering with SVB Leerink LLC and Cantor Fitzgerald & Company. This Sales Agreement, established on June 21, 2024 is going to allow Crinetics to sell from time to time as needed up to an aggregate of $350 million worth of shares of its common stock. The good news is that it hasn't yet tapped into this agreement, because as of the date of the 10-Q SEC filing it has sold no shares under it. The point here is that this remains a viable financing option for the company to raise some cash should it need to do so. The company burns roughly $130.1 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $80.3 million in R&D expenses and then $49.8 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks for investors to be aware of before investing in Crinetics Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the FDA approval of PALSONIFY that was just achieved for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. The first risk here is that despite the projected market expected to reach $2.37 billion by 2030 and beyond, there is no assurance that the company is going to be able to do well in terms of revenues for this candidate. Nor that the advantages of being an oral therapy and have greater control of breakthrough symptoms, is going to allow it to overcome competition from current SOC SRLs.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity that this company might have with respect to the development of paltusotine for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. This would entail the MAA of this drug for the treatment of these rare endocrine disorder patients. The CHMP is expected to give its opinion about potential European market approval for this drug in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this regulatory body is going to give a positive opinion for it for starters. Secondly, there is no guarantee that the European Medicines Agency [EMA] is going to approve this drug for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly thereafter.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the already initiated pivotal phase 3 CAREFNDR study, which is using paltusotine for the treatment of patients with carcinoid syndrome. Even though this oral SST2 Agonist has been shown to perform well in the targeting of adults with Acromegaly, there is no assurance that it will do well in these other patients with carcinoid syndrome. Thus, the risk here is that there is no guarantee that the primary endpoint of this late-stage trial, which is the change from baseline to week 12 in diary reported flushing episodes, is going to be met in a statistically significant manner. Nor that the data to be reported is going to be enough to warrant the filing of regulatory applications globally for potential marketing approval of it.

Conclusion

Crinetics Pharmaceuticals has done well to develop the use of paltusotine, to be marketed as PALSONIFY, for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly. The beauty of this is that even though FDA approval was achieved, this doesn't mean that there aren't other milestones for investors to keep an eye on in the next few years. For instance, as I noted above, the CHMP is expected to give its opinion on this drug for the treatment of these patients in the 1st half of 2026. However, this is just going to be an opinion and ultimately the European Commission [EC] is going to decide whether or not it should be approved.

The company is already looking for other ways to develop this oral SST2 Agonist, and this would be in terms of treating patients with carcinoid syndrome in the ongoing phase 3 CAREFNDR study. Everything I have described above is of great value for the company and to its shareholders. However, it has the potential to obtain value beyond the development of PALSONIFY. That is, it has another clinical candidate in its pipeline, which is oral ACTH Antagonist atumelnant.

The goal of this company is to initiate two pivotal phase 3 studies for this particular drug treating patients with CAH. The phase 3 CALM-CAH study is expected to recruit adults patients and then the phase 2/3 BALANCE-CAH trial is going to recruit only pediatric patients. Both of these late-stage trials are expected to kick off in the 2nd half of 2025. With FDA approval of PALSONIFY for the treatment of adults with Acromegaly, plus the ability to potentially expand its use for the targeting of patients with carcinoid syndrome, I believe that it is best to maintain the stock with a "Buy Rating".

data.title: BlackBerry: Slow Turnaround
data.description:
Over the last year, BlackBerry Limited (NYSE:BB) finally turned the corner. The intelligent software company has spent years trying to transition from a seller of smartphones to a focus on mobile and security software. My investment thesis is Neutral on the stock, as the recent gains more than account for any improvements in the business.

Improving Results

Blackberry reported an August quarter where the numbers were only impressive in comparison to past weak results. The company reported revenue growth of only 3% with a small profit as follows:

The QNX business has turned into a solid growth story with OS growth in connected cars and future potential in robots. BlackBerry is currently in 255 million vehicles with a growing backlog reaching $855 million in FY25.

The company forecasts this division to grow ~10% to reach $256-$270 million in the current fiscal year with likely guidance for solid growth in FY27. The royalty growth rate has slowed down with minimal growth during FY25 after exceptional growth in the prior couple of years, going from $460 million in FY22 to $815 million in FY24.

BlackBerry is still half reliant on the Secure Communications business where the FY26 revenue guidance is only at $239-$247 million after dipping YoY in FQ2. The segment saw FQ2 revenues slip by 10% to just $60 million while the ARR increased YoY to $213 million.

BlackBerry achieved these results while cutting costs. The company is now reporting a GAAP profit with operating expenses of $75 million in the quarter, down 5% from the prior year while investing in growing the QNX business.

The intelligent software company reported adjusted EBITDA of $26 million, up to a 20% margin and far above last year due to selling the Cylance division. BlackBerry has positive cash flows now, allowing the company to repurchase shares with the strong $364 million cash balance as of the end of August. In addition, the Cylance division was sold last December for $160 million in cash and 5.5 million common shares of Arctic Wolf.

No Longer A Discount

The stock now trades at 5x forward sales with a lot of the recent rally due to multiple expansion. Worth noting, BlackBerry actually reported sales of $145 million in FQ3'25 prior to dumping Cylance, so the reported number of $129 million was actually below the sales from last year.

BlackBerry is only forecast to grow at a mid-single digit clip in the years ahead. The QNX royalty business has some market opportunity in IoT and robotics areas where sales growth could accelerate; otherwise, the company appears set to plod along with minimal growth.

The company forecasts the SAM nearly doubling to $5.9 billion in CY28 due to the expansion into new areas beyond auto. BlackBerry has very much struggled to drive acceleration growth in these highly competitive areas in the past, so an investor should not assume the intelligent software company grabs a large market share in hot new areas like robotics.

Management guided revenue estimates for the year to $519-$541 million with analysts currently at a FY27 revenue target of $575 million. The stock still trades close to 5x EV/S targets for the next fiscal year, while the forecast is for minimal earnings.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that BlackBerry has made a solid investment over the last year when the stock was bought on dips. The company now needs substantial new growth drivers to play out to warrant further upside after the quick rally to nearly $5. If BlackBerry breaks above the prior highs from the end of June, the stock probably has some quick upside to the yearly high at $6 before taking a major pause.

data.title: Solana Steadies As Traders Weigh Inflows Against Technical Weakness
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Solana (SOL-USD) is trading near $196, stabilizing after a sharp sell-off from highs above $250 earlier this month. The token’s latest decline tested mid-channel support and the 100-day EMA near $194, a level now acting as a critical pivot for the broader bullish structure.

Whether buyers can hold this zone will determine if Solana avoids a deeper retracement toward $181 or even $160, where stronger historical support sits.

Technical structure at a crossroads

The daily chart shows Solana contained within a broad ascending channel, but momentum has shifted after repeated bearish breaks of structure. Resistance is now stacked at $208 and $219, followed by the $245–$250 ceiling that capped September’s rally. Failure to reclaim the $208–$210 range would leave the token vulnerable to renewed selling pressure.

SOL Price Dynamics (Source: TradingView)

The RSI has cooled from overbought levels and now sits in neutral territory, reflecting fading buyer conviction. While the long-term channel remains intact, short-term control lies with sellers unless buyers can decisively push back above the $210 threshold.

On-chain flows highlight mixed sentiment

Exchange data offers a nuanced view. On September 26, Solana registered positive net inflows of $21.6 million, coinciding with price stabilization around $196. This indicates dip-buying interest, though the broader pattern has been uneven, with significant outflows accompanying the decline from $250. Sustained positive flows will be key to offsetting profit-taking and reviving upward momentum.

Market participants remain cautious as liquidity conditions tighten. Without consistent inflows, traders may demand lower prices before committing to fresh accumulation, keeping the $181 zone in focus as the next test of demand.

Outlook

Fundamentally, Solana retains one of the most active ecosystems in blockchain, with vibrant DeFi and NFT activity anchoring long-term investor interest. However, the immediate trajectory is defined by technical positioning and liquidity shifts. If bulls can stabilize above the 100-day EMA and reclaim the $210 zone, the path toward retesting $245–$250 reopens. Otherwise, momentum risks sliding toward $181, where longer-term buyers may find value.

Previously, we noted Solana’s heavy selling pressure near $250 and highlighted the importance of structural support in guiding the next move. Today’s action reinforces that view, with the $193–$196 zone emerging as the market’s battleground. The next sessions will reveal whether Solana’s resilience can withstand sustained seller pressure.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: AMD: Love The Products, Hate The Share Price
data.description:
The past 5-year period would likely go down in history as one of the best times to own semiconductor stocks. Their importance within the economy and our daily lives would only increase from here on, but that doesn't mean that shareholders would enjoy the same kind of returns.

The reason why I say this is twofold. Firstly, during periods of sharp increases in demand, semiconductor companies have significant pricing power and that's exactly what has happened in recent years. As a result, we have witnessed unprecedented margin tailwinds that might not last in the coming years.

Secondly, valuations within the sector are hovering near record-high and they are already pricing in years of abnormal growth.

With all that in mind, one would assume that holding semiconductor stocks in recent years has been a highly rewarding experience. Without a doubt, this is certainly true for stocks like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), but it cannot be said for Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD).

Over the past 5-year period, AMD has delivered roughly the same returns as the S&P 500 Index (SP500), but with one big caveat—it was much more volatile than the equity index, and as such it significantly underperformed on a risk-adjusted basis.

And if this performance was what AMD had to offer during one of the best periods on record for semiconductor stocks, then what is left for periods when both growth rates and pricing power normalize? Not to mention any potential disruptions in the equity market or the global economy.

Throughout this period, AMD's share price was almost entirely influenced by changes in its valuation multiples, i.e., expectations about the future, broad market dynamics, etc. At the same time, the net income figure over the past 12-month period is just slightly different from what it was at the end of the year 2020—$2.8bn on a TTM basis vs. $2.5bn in FY 2020.

But after everything said so far, one could reasonably ask, why single out AMD in that regard? Most of the stuff said so far also applies to many other semiconductor stocks and AMD is without a doubt very well positioned as one of the leaders in both the GPU and x86 CPU markets. Thus, if we focus on the product roadmap alone then AMD surely stands at the very top in terms of attractiveness.

However, the attractiveness of a stock could differ materially from that of the company's product lineup, and that's exactly the reason why I single out AMD stock as one of the worst choices among the popular semiconductor names.

Why Single Out AMD?

It is quite surprising how consistently the market is pricing semiconductor stocks based on their ability to derive a price premium from their products. After all, the higher pricing power you have, the more likely it is that your chip/product is superior to those of competitors.

With that in mind, AMD appears to be fairly priced in once we plot gross margins against forward price/sales multiples within the expanded peer group. But that's only at the surface.

What ultimately matters for shareholders is whether or not your company can translate this high pricing power (high gross margin) into net income or cash flow when the level of accruals or leverage differs. But as we move down through the income statements of these semiconductor stocks we could see that their ability to derive high operating margins based on their pricing power differs materially.

Namely, three stocks stand out: Arm Holdings plc (ARM), Intel Corporation (INTC) and AMD. The reason why gross and operating margins of ARM differ so much is because of the company's royalty and licensing-based business model which allows for gross margins close to 100%. The case for Intel is a bit different with the company struggling to fend off competitors while at the same time trying to develop its Foundry business. But AMD stands out as a company that is supposedly going through one of its best periods in history and yet it is struggling to achieve high operating margins on a GAAP basis.

Things look slightly better if we account for non-GAAP adjustments, such as stock-based compensation expense and amortization of intangibles. But the fact of the matter is that AMD's Non-GAAP operating margin is still quite low—12% during the last reported quarter. That's nowhere near the last quarterly GAAP operating margin of NVDA of 61%.

Another issue that lingers around AMD is the relentless fall of its inventory turnover. On the graph below, we can see that even Intel's inventory turnover has rebounded from its 2022 drop and has largely trailed that of NVDA. For AMD, however, that was not the case, and the business continued to be far less efficient when it came to inventory levels. This could be attributed to the Xilinx acquisition, but it shows that large M&A deals do not come free and could point out some potential problems with the efficiency of the combined enterprise.

Valuation Premium That's Hard To Justify

So far, nothing suggests problems for AMD stock down the line. The logic dictates that the market should properly account for these differences between AMD and its peers and price the stock accordingly. But that does not seem to be the case.

For a company that is still struggling to achieve high operating margins on a GAAP basis, AMD is priced at a significant premium on a net income basis, even against the already generously priced stocks of Nvidia and Broadcom. As a matter of fact, NVDA looks like a bargain when plotted against AMD.

Things are no different even on a free cash flow basis. On the graph below, we can see how AMD's free cash flow yield fluctuated in recent years. In addition to its significant volatility, we also see that based on the current share price, AMD trades at a free cash flow yield of only 1.6%.

To put that into perspective, both NVDA and AVGO trade at roughly the same yields.

That mostly makes sense if we look at the product roadmaps in isolation and speculate about AMD's role in the high-growth Data Center market. However, even after years of high demand in that area, AMD comes nowhere near NVDA and AVGO in terms of Net Income margins and expected revenue growth—two profoundly important metrics for shareholders.

Investor Takeaway

Although momentum in semiconductor stocks shows no signs of cooling off, I find it hard to justify AMD's current share price. Even based on the currently optimistic assumptions about the business, the stock's relative pricing against other peers is hard to justify. Moreover, the probability of AMD achieving high GAAP margins over the next 5-year period appears quite low, given the company's unsuccessful efforts to do so during the exceptionally supportive environment in recent years. Therefore, even though very high investors' optimism and market momentum could support AMD's share price in the near term, I remain skeptical that current levels are sustainable.

data.title: These REITs Look Great As Fed Starts Cutting Cycle
data.description:
In a recent analysis, we discussed that Stephen Miran and follow-up appointees are likely to take the Fed Funds rate lower. This of course, has implications on much of the investment universe, but this article will be focused on the individual securities that stand to benefit the most. There are 3 main topics we want to cover:

  1. Will cuts to the short end of the interest rate curve impact the long end?
  2. Investment dollars pushed out the risk curve
  3. Interest expense savings of capital-intensive companies

Curve dynamics

The yield curve is typically considered to be upward sloping with a “normal” curve looking approximately like a graphed square root function. Longer duration bonds generally provide higher yields as compensation for greater duration risk.

In recent years however, we have had some atypical yield curve shapes. It was inverted for a while, where the long end was lower yield than the short end. Presently, it has a normal upward sloping shape from the 2-year to 30-year, but still has a bit of oddity where very short-term rates pay higher yields than the 2-year Treasury.

The short end of the curve is essentially forced to be above 4% because of the Fed Funds rate.

In September, the Fed lowered the Fed Funds rate to a range of 400 to 425 basis points.

When the Fed cuts like this, it almost immediately reduces the short end of the yield curve in parallel with the cut.

The impact of a Fed cut on the longer end of the yield curve is less clear and there is a good bit of debate on what will happen.

Some believe the long end will be unaffected because it is already somewhat steep relative to the 2-year. Thus, the short end would simply come down however many basis points were cut and the long end would remain.

I see this to be among the plausible scenarios. However, I think it is more likely that the long end will come down, just to a lesser degree.

Perhaps for each 50 basis points of cuts, the 10-year and 30-year would drop something like 20 basis points. I think it will be a consequence of capital forced out on the duration curve.

Presently there is a glut of capital in money market funds. $7.3 trillion is an unprecedented level for capital in money market funds.

I believe much of this capital is here to take advantage of unusually high short-term rates.

After years of getting almost no return on cash held in money market funds, the 4%+ available today feels excessively lucrative.

A 4% return that is close to risk free and almost fully liquid is a great deal. Market participants recognize this and have crowded in.

In a free market, crowding into a tranche would naturally lower the yield, but it is being artificially held up by the Fed Funds rate. The Fed Funds rate more or less dictates the minimum yield for the short end of the curve.

As evidence that there is a disproportionate amount of capital in short-term funds, observe the overall money supply below.

M2 has indeed expanded from about $15 trillion pre-pandemic to just over $22 trillion today.

That is an increase in money supply of about 47%.

In contrast, capital in Money market funds went from about $3 trillion pre-pandemic to about $7.3 trillion.

That is an increase of 143%.

If our previous analysis is correct and the Fed continues cutting, these money market investments will no longer be atypically lucrative. As such, the volume of capital in them would likely drop back down to a normal amount.

If we assume the pre-pandemic percentage of M2 in money market funds, that would be about 20% of capital or about $4.4 trillion.

This implies that the extra $2.9 trillion that is sitting in money market funds today would need to find a new home. Those who want to maintain the high yield they are currently enjoying would be forced in 1 of 2 directions:

  1. Further out the duration curve.
  2. Further out the risk curve.

To the extent capital moves from money markets into long duration Treasuries, it would pull long end yields down. A trillion dollars is enough capital to make a significant difference. For this reason, I believe Fed cuts will be reflected in the long end of the yield curve, just probably not in a full 1 to 1 parallel ratio.

The capital that instead chooses to move out on the risk curve could go into some combination of high yield corporate bonds, high dividend equities and preferred stocks.

We believe all 3 categories will benefit as the influx of perhaps a trillion dollars or more would be a boon to market prices. This is an event that has happened many times in the past. In the previous cycle, it was dubbed TINA or There Is No Alternative. With fewer risk-free options to attain a high yield, the remaining high yield securities become more attractive.

3 macroeconomic factors to watch for:

  1. Significantly reduced short-term interest rates
  2. Moderately reduced long-term interest rates
  3. Capital flows into high yield securities.

Each of these points to certain equities and preferreds that stand to disproportionately benefit.

Gladstone Commercial (GOOD) savings on interest expense

Gladstone Commercial has significant borrowings on their credit facility that is pegged to SOFR.

SOFR tracks with the Fed Funds rate, so as cuts come in, GOOD’s interest expense declines.

GOOD has $462 million of variable rate debt, so each 25 basis point cut, including the September cut reduces run-rate of annual interest expense by $1.156 million.

That is about 2.5 cents of FFO per share accretion per 25 basis point Fed cut.

The market is anticipating 2 more cuts in 2025.

Based on our previous analysis, I agree with the market that October and December cuts are likely.

If there are indeed 3 cuts (including the September cut) that represents 7.5 cents of FFO and AFFO accretion for GOOD on a per share basis. That is significant savings that secures their outsized 9.5% dividend yield.

High debt will be less punished

REITs with high debt loads have suffered in the high interest rate environment. The higher the debt relative to equity the more their interest expense took a bite out of earnings as interest rates rose.

Most REITs had well termed out debt so the rise to interest expense was not immediate, but the high interest rate environment lasted many years such that most debt eventually had to roll to the now higher rates.

That is already starting to reverse as long-term interest rates have dropped significantly due to spread compression. To the extent that longer term rates come down in tandem with cuts long term rates could decline even further.

We have already observed many REITs able to issue 8+ year debt at 5% or less. That is a rate which allows for a healthy spread on investing the proceeds in properties.

Most capital-intensive businesses stand to benefit from cheaper financing, but it disproportionately benefits those with higher debt loads relative to total capital. It may be tempting to run a screen for high debt to EBITDA companies to find those with the most benefit. However, one has to be careful that the figure is not the result of simply having low EBITDA. Most of the companies that populate the top of this screen are up here for wonky reasons.

Apartment Investment and Management (AIV) for example, just has really high debt to EBITDA because a large portion of their portfolio is in development and thus not flowing with EBITDA.

Instead, what we are looking to pinpoint here are companies with ample EBITDA that simply operate toward the higher end of leverage. Specifically we are looking for strong, growing companies with high debt loads.

NexPoint Residential (NXRT) fits the bill with a long history of organic growth in its Sunbelt apartments. They simply like to operate at high leverage with a debt to EBITDA of 9.39X.

A significant portion of NXRT’s debt is hedged to be functionally fixed rate so they would not get the immediate interest expense savings like Gladstone Commercial, but over a few years, as debt rolls, NXRT is positioned to enjoy significant AFFO accretion from lower interest rates.

Capital flows into fixed income investments

The extra roughly $3 trillion currently sitting in money market funds above the normal amount may have to find a new home. As discussed earlier, some of it will flow to longer duration treasuries, but those looking to maintain a higher yield may seek out preferreds.

The REIT preferred market is relatively small so it would not take all that much capital to really move market prices. Indeed we observed in the previous cycle when the influx of capital sent most fixed rate preferreds with yields in excess of 6% to above par.

This cycle is different as interest rates are unlikely to go as low as they were in 2021. As such, I think the threshold to be bid up to over par is closer to 7% yield.

At Portfolio Income Solutions we track the live pricing of equity and mortgage REIT preferreds to monitor them for discount to par, current yield and fundamental dividend security factors.

There are dozens of REIT preferreds with yields in excess of 8%, many of which will potentially be bid up to or above par. The key to maximizing returns in this potentially tailwind environment is to pay close attention to discount to par.

2 different preferreds may be trading at 8% current yields, but if one of those has an 8% coupon it is already at $25 par and has minimal room to appreciate as threat of redemption prevents it from going much over $25.

However, if there is a 6% coupon preferred trading at an 8% current yield it has a large upside. As interest rates decline, this preferred might be bid up to a 7% current yield at which point it would still be discounted to par so there would be no threat of redemption capping its upside.

There are over 10 opportunities of this nature presently and as an example we can look at Pebblebrook Preferred H. (PEB) (PEB.PR.H)

PEB is a highly respected and long-tenured hotel REIT which is how they were able to issue preferreds at coupons in the low 6s and high 5s.

As environmental interest rates increased these low coupon preferreds started to trade at very large discounts to par. PEB-H is trading at $18.41 against a par value of $25 because the market has decided a fair current yield in the current environment is 7.74%.

In an environment resembling the TINA of the past returns, where there is a scarcity of high quality high yield securities, we believe that equilibrant market yield will be closer to 6.5%. To get there, PEB-H would have to trade at $21.92.

That would be 19% capital appreciation on top of the 7.74% yield we are paid to wait.

Wrapping it up

As interest rates move, each security is impacted differently. Some benefit; others are harmed. The examples of opportunity presented in this article are just the tip of the iceberg. They can serve as a template of the concepts that signal disproportionate benefit.

data.title: U.S. Core PCE Inflation Rises As Expected
data.description:
By Christian Norman

August PCE Highlights

  • US Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) August YoY (ex. Food & Energy): +2.9% vs +2.9% expected, meets consensus
  • US Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) August MoM (ex. Food & Energy): +2% vs +0.2% expected, meets consensus
  • US Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) August YoY: +2.7% vs +2.7% expected, meets consensus
  • US Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) August MoM: +0.3% vs +0.3% expected, meets consensus
  • US Personal Income August MoM: +0.4% vs +0.3% expected, above consensus by +0.1%
  • US Personal Spending August MoM: +0.6% vs +0.5% expected, above consensus by +0.1%

US Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Report (August 2025):

US PCE Report (August 2025), U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis

Breaking: US core PCE rises by 2.9% YoY in August, up 0.2% MoM. The report broadly meets expectations with PCE numbers all meeting consensus, while personal income and spending came in marginally higher.

Key takeaway: The Federal Reserve’s ‘preferred’ inflation gauge, personal consumption expenditures (PCE), is rising in line with expectations. Meeting consensus in today’s report, should inflation also remain under control by other metrics, this would support the notion of further easing efforts.

“From the preceding month, the PCE price index for August increased 0.3 percent. Excluding food and energy, the PCE price index increased 0.2 percent.

Personal income increased $95.7 billion (0.4 percent at a monthly rate) in August, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $86.1 billion (0.4 percent) and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $129.2 billion (0.6 percent).

Personal outlays—the sum of PCE, personal interest payments, and personal current transfer payments—increased $132.9 billion in August. Personal saving was $1.06 trillion in August and the personal saving rate—personal saving as a percentage of disposable personal income—was 4.6 percent.“

US Personal Income and Outlays, August 2025, U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis

Market Reaction

EUR/USD, OANDA,TradingView, 26/09/2025

Since the release, EUR/USD remains relatively unchanged, having recovered losses made in the minutes after. Otherwise, Gold (XAU/USD) trades 0.06% higher, while Dow futures are up 0.45% ahead of the NYSE open.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Marvell: Undervalued With Revenue Growth That Outpaces Peers
data.description:
Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL) is a high-growth semiconductor company that designs and supplies high-performance technology for data-intensive applications, such as artificial intelligence. The company also offers semiconductor solutions for carrier infrastructure, enterprise networks, industrials, automotive, and others.

MRVL stock is down roughly 30% year-to-date. This represents significant broader market underperformance, which I think was mainly driven by overly inflated multiples. The stock trades at a forward P/E of about 29, indicating a 12% premium versus the sector median of approximately 25x, which is justified by a roughly 5x higher revenue growth rate of 37% versus peers' nearly 7% over the past 12 months.

I think Marvell is well-positioned to benefit from continued AI demand. The stock appears oversold and may rebound from these levels. Marvell Technology has been a phenomenal top and bottom line growth story so far, and I think tailwinds tied to its business model may boost multiple expansion.

I rate Marvell Technology as a Strong Buy with a $98 price target by fiscal FY2027, implying 18% upside potential and broader market outperformance. I think MRVL appears to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors who seek a fairly valued AI play with strong demand and a positive outlook.

Q2 2026: The Bottom Line Was In Line, And $4 Million Top-Line Miss Shouldn't Cloud 57% Revenue Growth

On August 28, the company disclosed FQ2 2026 results. The market was not impressed by the report, as the company faced a significant, approximately 19% selloff over the following trading day. However, since then, the company has fully rebounded, underscoring that the initial reaction may have been overly pessimistic.

I think that too — the company reported in line with market expectations diluted EPS of $0.67, representing more than 120% growth on a year-over-year basis from $0.30, the same quarter last year. The top-line came in at $2.01 billion, a massive 57% increase, despite a roughly $4 million miss in revenue, reflecting a minor 0.2% surprise. These results indicate substantial performance and great execution from management. I think that the company, growing its top and bottom line at such numbers, may be rewarded with a higher premium.

Management highlighted that their data center revenue has increased by an impressive 69% over the past 12 months to $1.49 billion. This represents 74% of total consolidated revenue, implying significant demand for MRVL technology. The company also highlighted significant recovery in enterprise and carrier segments, with their combined revenue increasing 43% on a year-over-year basis.

Overall, this has been a strong quarter for the company, which supports the bullish thesis for the stock. The company experiences significant demand in different segments, and I think this deserves a premium valuation.

Management has also provided guidance for the upcoming quarter. MRVL anticipates revenue to come in at $2.06 billion at the midpoint, with roughly 59.75% gross margin (non-GAAP). The company also expects $0.74 diluted EPS at the midpoint, which would imply nearly a 70% increase on a year-over-year basis. Marvell Technologies anticipates a continuation of demand, which should drive top and bottom lines higher.

I think these expectations are rational, and I anticipate high double-digit growth from the company. This is also supported by other semiconductors, such as Micron Technology, which delivered a significant quarterly report on September 23, indicating strong, AI-driven demand.

Marvell Technologies appears to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors who seek a high-growth stock at undervalued levels. If management continues to execute at such a pace, I think it is likely that the market will reprice the stock at a higher premium.

MRVL May Appear Fairly Priced, But At This Growth Rate, I Could Argue It Is Actually Cheap

The company may appear fairly priced at the forward P/E of 28.59, implying an 11.83% overvaluation compared to the sector median of 25.10, but it is not. As advised, MRVL has been a phenomenal top-line story so far, with a 37.05% revenue growth rate over the past 12 months, which should be rewarded with a higher premium.

Although the company is trading at a roughly 15% premium to the S&P 500 at 23.57x, too, it is growing its top-line by 3.7x faster than the average company in the benchmark at 10%. There is a significant disconnection in valuations, which I think may be repriced by investors. This also indicates that if the management continues to execute, there is room for multiple expansions.

This table above supports my thesis. 9 months ago, the company was trading at above a 75x earnings multiple. I do not think it will visit these levels any time soon, but it shows that MRVL used to trade comfortably above a 30x earnings multiple over the past years. I think investors may continue pricing the company at a premium, as they may be willing to pay extra for a high-quality stock with significant demand.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 0.87, the company appears to be about 53% discounted to the industry's average of 1.83. This supports my bullish narrative and indicates that MRVL appears to be a relatively cheap stock.

The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 8.92 and 8.48, respectively, offset the bullish outlook and indicate that the company's future growth may already be priced in. The stock trades at roughly 2.5x higher at the midpoint versus peers of 3.52 for both metrics.

Marvell Technology appears to be a medium-leveraged company with $4.78 billion in total obligations. The company has roughly $1.22 billion in cash that partially offsets the debt. However, this balances the bullish outlook, as a high-interest environment is not favorable for the stock. Last quarter, MRVL paid $51.9 million in interest expense, and if high interest rates remain for longer, this could significantly impact the company's bottom line. On the other hand, if the Fed continues lowering interest rates, MRVL may be able to refinance its obligations on more favorable conditions.

This semiconductor stock is not only a revenue growth story but a bottom-line one, too. The diluted EPS forward growth rate of roughly 31% indicates nearly 2.8x outperformance compared to peers of 11% and suggests the trend may continue. If the company can grow its bottom line at such a substantial pace further, valuations may appear even more compelling.

The EBITDA grew by 112% over the past 12 months, reflecting 11x outperformance versus the sector median of 10%. The forward EBIT growth rate of 28% implies about 2.4x greater metric than peers of 12%. These results are phenomenal and boost my bullish thesis for the stock.

Marvell Technology operates at a market-like gross profit margin (TTM) of 49.59%, versus peers of 49.48%. The EBITDA margin (TTM) of 11% is roughly 75% higher than the industry's average of 6%.

On the downside, the net income margin (TTM) of negative 1.43% is significantly below the industry's average of 4.28%. This could support the bearish narrative for the company, but so far, it has been a phenomenal revenue growth story, and I think that investors should focus on significant demand rather than worry too much about the bottom line.

The company heavily invests in its future growth, and the following chart of capital expenditures (TTM) reflects that argument. I believe this is a correct capital allocation strategy, as the company is positioning itself for a dominant stance in the high-growth market in the years ahead.

The company has generated a substantial $1.84 billion in cash from operations (TTM), which reflects massive outperformance compared to peers of only $120 million.

Marvell is a high-quality company that is operating at a market-like gross margin. The semiconductor landscape experiences significant, AI-driven demand, and MRVL is well-positioned to benefit from its growth. Based on the meaningful revenue growth in high digits, it seems that MRVL technology is not only a high-quality but also critical for data centers and high-performance, data-driven solutions.

I think the trend may continue. The global data center market was valued at roughly $242 billion in 2024 and is expected to reach about $585 billion by 2032. This implies 11.7% CAGR. MRVL results underscore the massive need for the company's technologies, and with its massive investments in capital expenditures, management is positioning itself to benefit from the broader market growth.

Wall Street forecasts $3.38 in EPS for FY2027. I think this is a realistic estimate, considering significant AI-driven semiconductor technology demand. The company was able to steadily grow its top and bottom line over the past years, and I think the trend is likely to continue.

If we apply the current earnings multiple at 29x for the forecasted EPS, we come in at my price target of around $98. I think this projection could come in conservative, given the growth tailwinds, but it also leaves room for error. This earnings multiple would indicate a slightly elevated valuation for the high-growth name. However, if management continues to substantially increase its top-line growth, I think investors could reprice MRVL at an even higher premium.

Marvell Technology appears to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors. Despite massive growth, the stock price has consolidated for almost a year, and now it may be well-positioned to rebound from here.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Marvell Technology, which I think could impact the stock price performance over the short term.

I think that the main concern is related to saturated revenue. 74% of total consolidated revenue comes from data center-related business. If the market experiences any weakness or slower-than-anticipated growth, the company may continue trading at market-like valuation, despite significantly higher results. However, if the top line is impacted, too, sentiment may shift, and AI-related names could correct.

A lot of companies are disclosing heavy investments in capital expenditures and AI infrastructure. However, it has not translated into meaningful revenue yet. If the broader market struggles to monetize AI infrastructure, sentiment may shift dramatically, and AI-related names could also decline in stock price.

The company competes with fierce names, such as Broadcom. Larger companies may have stronger integration solutions and deeper resources, which may make it difficult to compete against them. If MRVL struggles to challenge and may lose market share, this could result in slower-than-anticipated growth, and the earnings multiple may contract.

Marvell Technology is exposed to tariff risk, too. If trade tensions escalate, the company may face limitations in accessing specific markets or increased costs. This could materially hurt both top and bottom line projections.

However, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, MRVL may be well-positioned to benefit from AI-driven demand. The company offers a compelling, high-quality product, which is essential for AI infrastructure, and substantial demand supports that.

Conclusion: Undervalued AI Play, With Potential For Market Outperformance

Marvell Technology appears to be a compelling, undervalued AI play, which I think could outperform the broader market in the short term. MRVL experiences significant demand in the data center and carrier infrastructure segments, which makes it an interesting long-term opportunity.

The company operates at a market-like gross profit margin and heavily invests in its future growth. MRVL appears to be a medium-leveraged company, which partially clouds the bullish outlook, but with current high double-digit top and bottom line growth, it seems to be trading at an undervalued level, presenting an appealing upside potential.

I rate Marvell Technology as a Strong Buy with a $98 price target. The company has several tailwinds ahead, and I think it is well-positioned to benefit from global data center market growth.

42 Wall Street analysts predict a $86 price target over the next 12 months, offering a 3.5% upside. I am more bullish. I think at current levels, the market may be mispricing the stock, as such significant execution from management may be rewarded with a higher premium. Time will tell, but MRVL appears to be a compelling long-term entry for investors who may seek AI exposure.

data.title: KBWB: Banks Cruising In 2025, Leading The DJIA, Reiterate "Buy"
data.description:
The two best stocks in the Dow Jones Industrial Average so far this year are a pair of banks. Goldman Sachs (GS) earns the crown, up 40%, dividends included, while JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is the runner-up, higher by 32% in 2025 as we approach the end of the third quarter. Those two are among the top three holdings in the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (NASDAQ:KBWB).

I had a "Buy" rating on the fund back in the second quarter, when new rules around gold’s classification in the new Basel III framework were seen as potentially bullish for domestic financial institutions, at least in my view.

KBWB has returned a very strong 19.4% since then, outperforming the S&P 500 by 7 percentage points. I reiterate a "Buy" rating. While the valuation is higher, and the ETF’s relative strength has eased, I still see more upside.

KBWB's Alpha Trend Has Stalled Somewhat

According to the issuer, KBWB "is based on the KBW Nasdaq Bank Index. The ETF will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in the securities that comprise the underlying index." Using "a modified-market cap-weighted index of companies primarily engaged in U.S. banking activities," the fund is composed of large national U.S. money centers, regional banks, and thrift institutions that are publicly traded in the United States. KBWB is "rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly."

KBWB is a medium-sized ETF with $5.6 billion in assets under management as of Sept. 25, 2025. That’s up significantly from just $3.8 billion at the time of my previous assessment. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about 0.7 percentage points higher than that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been robust throughout the year, earning KBWB a solid A ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system (it was an A+ three months ago). Still, KBWB can be risky at times, as evidenced by a high standard deviation of 25.7%. The fund also exhibits elevated tracking error.

Finally, on liquidity, KBWB scores well thanks to its high average daily volume of more than 2.2 million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just a single basis point, per Invesco.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has high exposure to US large-cap value. There is no international exposure, and zero access to the growth style. So, much hinges on the macro landscape, and not so much on compelling narratives around tech. Still, there’s some size diversification due to the 43% allocation to domestic SMID caps.

On valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio has increased by about three figures. That’s a large jump, which does concern me. But with a long-term earnings growth rate of 11.7%, the resulting PEG ratio is still barely above 1x.

KBWB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As hinted at earlier, GS and JPM are two stellar performers so far this year. That has helped KBWB generate significant alpha, even with the Mag 7 outperforming since mid-April. I’m also encouraged by what could be a record-high dividend payout. In 2025, we’ve seen banks be able to release capital reserves, which often flow through to shareholders via quarterly dividends.

In all the “Fab 5” Financials-sector banks comprise about 40% of the total allocation.

Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, we are about to embark on what’s usually a very bullish period. Over the past 10 years, KBWB has rallied significantly in October and November—the best two-month stretch on the calendar.

KBWB: Bullish Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a still-favorable valuation and healthy seasonal considerations, KBWB’s technical situation looks just fine to me. Notice in the chart below that shares broke through the resistance I noted last time in May. Given the upside advance, a measured move price objective to $93 is now in play based on the $21 depth of the February through April drawdown and subsequent rally through $72 around the turn of the year.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. I also like that there’s now a high amount of volume by price below the current level. A concern, however, is that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has printed lower highs as price has ascended. That's bearish divergence in technical analysis parlance, and it bears watching into year-end.

KBWB Upside Breakout Targets $93, Rising 200dma, Weaker RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on KBWB. U.S. large-cap banks have performed very well this year, though their relative and absolute momentum have eased. Still, with a modest valuation and constructive chart, I expect further gains.
